message:
feat(db): add v4 composite indexes for hot-path query optimization (br-15dv.1.5)

Add 5 composite indexes via v4 migration targeting the most expensive
query patterns at scale: ack-required views, thread retrieval, urgent
message filtering, and bidirectional contact lookups. Run ANALYZE after
index creation to update query planner statistics.

Indexes added:
- idx_mr_agent_ack: message_recipients(agent_id, ack_ts)
- idx_msg_thread_created: messages(thread_id, created_ts)
- idx_msg_project_importance_created: messages(project_id, importance, created_ts)
- idx_al_a_agent_status: agent_links(a_project_id, a_agent_id, status)
- idx_al_b_agent_status: agent_links(b_project_id, b_agent_id, status)

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.6 <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -100,7 +100,7 @@
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.2","title":"Read cache overhaul: LRU eviction, larger capacity, adaptive TTL","description":"PROBLEM: Cache caps at 1000 entries/category with 60s agent TTL. At 1000+ agents, every agent insertion triggers eviction check (cache.rs:161-168), cache hit rate drops to ~0%, and every tool call hits the DB.

CURRENT CODE (cache.rs:20-21):
  AGENT_TTL = 60s
  MAX_ENTRIES_PER_CATEGORY = 1000

SOLUTION: Complete cache overhaul:
1. Increase MAX_ENTRIES_PER_CATEGORY to 16,384 (still <2MB memory)
2. Increase AGENT_TTL to 300s (agents making calls every few minutes stay cached)
3. Implement true LRU eviction using intrusive linked list (not just capacity check)
4. Add adaptive TTL: frequently-accessed entries get longer TTL (up to 600s)
5. Add cache warming on startup: pre-load all agents for projects in STORAGE_ROOT
6. Add cache hit/miss ratio metrics (atomic counters, zero-lock)
7. Replace HashMap with IndexMap for O(1) LRU eviction

MEMORY BUDGET: 16K entries × 200 bytes avg = 3.2MB (acceptable for 1000-agent workload)

VALIDATION: stress_cache_coherency test must pass with 2000 agents and 30s agent TTL.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/cache.rs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:00.865739723Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T01:47:36.735835855Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T01:47:36.735809295Z","close_reason":"Cache overhaul complete: IndexMap LRU, 16K capacity, 300s agent TTL, adaptive TTL, per-category atomic metrics, warm_agents/warm_projects bulk insert, 19 unit tests + stress test passing, clippy clean","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["cache","db","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.2","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:00.865739723Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.2","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:54.244253682Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.3","title":"Shard deferred touch queue into 16 lock-independent segments","description":"PROBLEM: The deferred touch queue uses a single Mutex<HashMap<i64, i64>> (cache.rs:210-232). Every tool call acquires this mutex to record agent activity. At 100 concurrent tool calls/sec, this serializes all requests through one lock.

CURRENT CODE (cache.rs:214-224):
  let mut touches = self.deferred_touches.lock().unwrap_or_else(...)
  touches.insert(agent_id, ts_micros);

SOLUTION: Shard the deferred touch queue to eliminate contention:
1. Replace single Mutex<HashMap> with 16 shards: [Mutex<HashMap<i64, i64>>; 16]
2. Shard key: agent_id % 16 (deterministic, even distribution)
3. Each flush cycle drains all shards independently
4. Consider using parking_lot::Mutex for smaller/faster mutex (12 bytes vs 40 bytes)
5. Alternative: use DashMap (concurrent HashMap) if parking_lot isn't available

EXPECTED IMPROVEMENT: 16x reduction in lock contention on touch path.

VALIDATION: stress_deferred_touch_batch_correctness with 100 threads must show no regressions.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/cache.rs","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.006492153Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T02:14:49.770918923Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T02:14:49.770894347Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","locks","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.3","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.006492153Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.3","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.2","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:54.380002086Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.4","title":"Lock-free query tracker with atomic counters and pre-computed table IDs","description":"PROBLEM: The query tracker locks a global Mutex on EVERY database query (tracking.rs:100-130). It also runs extract_table() regex on every query. At 500 queries/sec (1000 agents × 5 queries/tool_call × 10% active), this is a massive serialization point.

CURRENT CODE (tracking.rs:110-113):
  let mut inner = self.inner.lock().unwrap_or_else(...)
  // update per-table counters
  // check slow query threshold

SOLUTION: Lock-free redesign:
1. Replace per-table Mutex<HashMap<String, Counter>> with array of AtomicU64 counters
   - Pre-allocate slots for known tables (projects, agents, messages, etc.)
   - Use enum TableId -> usize index for O(1) lookup
2. Use thread-local slow query buffers, periodically merged into global log
3. Pre-compute table extraction: embed table name in query metadata at call site instead of parsing SQL at runtime
4. Make tracking opt-in per query (not every query needs tracking)
5. Add separate AtomicU64 for total_queries, total_duration_us (no lock needed)

EXPECTED IMPROVEMENT: Zero lock contention on query hot path. O(1) atomic increment per query.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/tracking.rs, crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.157527907Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.157527907Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","locks","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.4","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.157527907Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-15dv.1.5","title":"Add missing composite indexes for hot-path queries (v4 migration)","description":"PROBLEM: Several hot-path queries lack optimal indexes, causing full table scans at scale.

MISSING INDEXES IDENTIFIED:
1. message_recipients(agent_id, ack_ts) — views/ack-required scans all recipients for an agent
2. messages(thread_id, created_at) — thread queries sort by timestamp
3. file_reservations(project_id, agent_id, expires_at) — force-release scans by project+agent
4. messages(project_id, importance, created_at) — urgent-unread view filters by importance
5. agent_links(a_project_id, a_agent_id, status) — contact list queries

EXISTING INDEXES (schema.rs:78-100):
  idx_message_recipients_agent(agent_id)
  idx_message_recipients_agent_message(agent_id, message_id)
  idx_file_reservations_project(project_id)
  idx_agents_project_name(project_id, name)

SOLUTION: Add v4 migration with covering indexes for hot-path queries:
1. CREATE INDEX idx_mr_agent_ack ON message_recipients(agent_id, ack_ts)
2. CREATE INDEX idx_msg_thread_created ON messages(thread_id, created_at)
3. CREATE INDEX idx_fr_project_agent_expires ON file_reservations(project_id, agent_id, expires_at)
4. CREATE INDEX idx_msg_project_importance ON messages(project_id, importance, created_at)
5. CREATE INDEX idx_al_a_status ON agent_links(a_project_id, a_agent_id, status)
6. Run ANALYZE after creating indexes

VALIDATION: EXPLAIN QUERY PLAN for each hot-path query must show index usage.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/schema.rs (v4 migration)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.304697522Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T03:47:46.134430523Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T03:47:46.134395788Z","close_reason":"Added v4 migration with 5 composite indexes + ANALYZE: idx_mr_agent_ack, idx_msg_thread_created, idx_msg_project_importance_created, idx_al_a_agent_status, idx_al_b_agent_status. Substituted file_reservations index (already well-covered) with b-side agent_links composite. All indexes also added to CREATE_TABLES_SQL for fresh DBs. 2 new tests, all 7 schema tests pass, 893+ workspace tests pass, 0 clippy warnings.","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","indexes","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.5","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.304697522Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.6","title":"PRAGMA tuning for high-concurrency 1000-agent workload","description":"PROBLEM: SQLite PRAGMAs materially affect throughput and tail latency under high concurrency, but naive tuning can easily create new failure modes (memory blowups, long checkpoints, IO spikes).

CURRENT STATE:
- PRAGMAs are applied per connection via a multi-statement block in `schema.rs`.
- Settings include WAL + synchronous=NORMAL + busy_timeout, plus cache_size/mmap_size/temp_store/threads.

CRITICAL NUANCE (do not forget):
- `PRAGMA cache_size` is effectively **per connection**. Increasing pool max_connections (br-15dv.1.1) multiplies total cache memory.
- `mmap_size` is configured per connection (the OS may share pages, but the virtual mapping can still be large).

SOLUTION: PRAGMA tuning as a *budgeted, measured* change set:
1. Define a memory budget for DB + page cache in br-15dv.10 terms (e.g., <= 512MB total on a dev box).
2. Derive per-connection cache_size from max_connections (e.g., 8-32MB per connection, not 512MB).
3. Tune WAL checkpoint behavior for bursty writes:
   - wal_autocheckpoint
   - journal_size_limit
   - explicit checkpoint strategy (on idle / shutdown / export)
4. Consider splitting read/write pool (br-15dv.1.1) and allocating more cache to the write pool if needed.
5. Add a benchmark + stress validation loop:
   - compare p95/p99 latencies and throughput before/after
   - ensure no regression under sustained write bursts

DELIVERABLES:
- A documented PRAGMA set for the target load model.
- Bench artifacts showing improvement (or revert if not).

VALIDATION:
- Bench suite shows >= 10% improvement on at least one bottleneck scenario without worsening p99.
- No memory explosion when max_connections increases.

- crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/pool.rs","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.447651584Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:41:59.012581775Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","perf","sqlite"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.6","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.447651584Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.6","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:54.772614333Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.7","title":"Static config caching with OnceLock to eliminate per-request env parsing","description":"PROBLEM: Config::from_env() appears to be called per-request in tool handlers (messaging.rs:346, reservations.rs:281). This parses 40+ environment variables, does string conversions, and allocates on every single tool call.

CURRENT PATTERN (in tool handlers):
  let config = Config::from_env();
  // use config...

SOLUTION: Cache config in a global static:
1. Add OnceLock<Config> global singleton
2. Config::get() returns &'static Config (zero-cost after first call)
3. Add Config::reload() for runtime reconfiguration (acquires write lock, replaces config)
4. Make Config fields public but immutable (no interior mutability needed)
5. Remove all Config::from_env() calls in tool handlers, replace with Config::get()

EXPECTED IMPROVEMENT: Eliminate ~40 env var lookups + string allocations per tool call.

VALIDATION: All conformance tests must pass. Config changes via env should apply on reload.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-core/src/config.rs, crates/mcp-agent-mail-tools/src/*.rs","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.593309799Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.593309799Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["config","perf"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.7","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.593309799Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.1.8","title":"Prepared statement cache for hot-path SQL queries","description":"PROBLEM: SQLite re-parses and re-plans every SQL query on every execution. At 500 queries/sec, the parser/planner overhead is significant.

SOLUTION: Add prepared statement caching:
1. Create PreparedStatementCache per connection (HashMap<&'static str, PreparedStatement>)
2. For hot-path queries (ensure_project, resolve_agent, fetch_inbox, send_message), use static SQL strings as cache keys
3. On cache miss, prepare + cache. On cache hit, rebind parameters and execute.
4. Clear cache on connection recycle (every 30 minutes)
5. Integration with sqlmodel_rust: check if prepare_cached() or similar API exists
6. If sqlmodel_rust doesn't support this, implement at the queries.rs level with raw connection access

NOTE: This depends on sqlmodel_rust API. May need upstream changes to /dp/sqlmodel_rust.

EXPECTED IMPROVEMENT: 30-50% reduction in query execution overhead for repeated queries.

VALIDATION: Benchmark suite must show measurable improvement in tool latency.

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/src/queries.rs, /dp/sqlmodel_rust (if needed)","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.741345556Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:54.509808091Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["db","perf","sqlite"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.8","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:27:01.741345556Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.1.8","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:54.509756234Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
@@ -156,7 +156,7 @@
 {"id":"br-15dv.8.7","title":"Git/archive IO metrics: commit latency + lock wait histograms","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T05:46:02.408902365Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T05:46:28.444682959Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["git","observability","storage"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.8.7","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.8","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T05:46:02.408902365Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.8.7","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.8.6","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T05:46:12.559131927Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":16,"issue_id":"br-15dv.8.7","author":"Dicklesworthstone","text":"## Background
At 1000+ concurrent agents, the archive path becomes a primary tail-latency driver:
- `.archive.lock` contention serializes per-project writes.
- `git` commit/index operations can stall on `index.lock`.
- Commit coalescing can hide backlog until it explodes.

Without first-class metrics, we are blind to whether p99 latency is dominated by:
- lock waits,
- git CPU time,
- filesystem flushes,
- queue backlog.

## Goal
Add low-overhead metrics that make archive/git contention observable and actionable in both:
- `resource://tooling/metrics_core` (always-on snapshot)
- `health_check` / diagnostics (summarized warnings)

## Proposed Metrics (Minimal but Sufficient)
### Histograms
- `storage.archive_lock_wait_us` (time spent waiting to acquire `projects/<slug>/.archive.lock`)
- `storage.commit_lock_wait_us` (time spent waiting for commit/index lock)
- `storage.git_commit_latency_us` (time spent performing commit/index update)

### Counters
- `storage.git_index_lock_retries_total`
- `storage.git_index_lock_failures_total`
- `storage.commit_attempts_total`
- `storage.commit_failures_total`

### Gauges
- `storage.commit_queue_depth` (current items pending)
- `storage.commit_batch_size_last` + optional rolling max

## Implementation Notes
- Instrument inside `crates/mcp-agent-mail-storage/src/lib.rs` at the lock acquisition boundaries, not around the entire tool call.
- Prefer monotonic timers; record in micros.
- Keep the hot path lock-free: use the metrics-core atomics/histograms (no HashMap/Mutex).

## Tests
- Unit test: metrics snapshot after a simulated lock acquisition includes the new keys and is JSON-serializable.
- Integration test (lightweight): run a minimal archive init + write op, then assert counters/histograms are non-zero.

## Acceptance Criteria
- Metrics appear in `resource://tooling/metrics_core` under a stable schema.
- No conformance fixture regressions.
- Overhead is amortized to ~O(atomic) per event; no new global locks.
","created_at":"2026-02-07T05:46:28Z"}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.9","title":"[track] Stress test expansion: 1000-agent load simulation and chaos testing","description":"Current stress tests use 2-16 threads with 10-200 operations—far below the 1000-agent target. No tests for: sustained high RPS, cache thrashing under agent churn, queue saturation, large message payloads (>1MB), or fault injection. Without these tests, we can't validate that optimizations actually work at scale. This track adds comprehensive load tests that simulate realistic 1000-agent scenarios and chaos/fault injection.","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:25:37.138878668Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:25:37.138878668Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["scale","stress","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9","depends_on_id":"br-15dv","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:25:37.138878668Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
 {"id":"br-15dv.9.1","title":"1000-agent concurrent stress test with 16K operations","description":"PROBLEM: Current stress tests max out at 16 threads with 200 operations. This is 60x below the 1000-agent target. We cannot validate that optimizations work at target scale without a test at target scale.

SOLUTION: 1000-agent concurrent connection stress test:
1. Spawn 1000 async tasks (using asupersync runtime)
2. Each task: ensure_project → register_agent → 5x send_message → 5x fetch_inbox → 5x ack
3. Total: 1000 registrations + 5000 sends + 5000 fetches + 5000 acks = 16,000 operations
4. Assertions:
   - Zero errors (no pool timeouts, no circuit breaks)
   - All 5000 messages retrievable
   - All 5000 acks recorded
   - Total duration < 120 seconds
   - Pool utilization peak < 90%
5. Run against in-memory SQLite (for CI speed) AND on-disk SQLite (for realism)
6. Record: operations/sec, p50/p95/p99 latency, peak memory RSS
7. Output results to tests/artifacts/stress/ for regression tracking
8. Must run in CI without special hardware (single machine, 4 cores)

DEPENDS ON: br-15dv.1.1 (pool sizing) — test will fail with default 7-connection pool

FILES: crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/tests/stress.rs (extend existing)","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:25.961133625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-08T02:07:14.205705155Z","closed_at":"2026-02-08T02:07:14.205672855Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["scale","stress","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:57.329642559Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.10","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:39:01.665177744Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.1","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:25.961133625Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
-{"id":"br-15dv.9.2","title":"Sustained 100 RPS load test for 300 seconds with stability assertions","description":"PROBLEM: Current tests measure burst performance (do N operations as fast as possible). They don't test sustained throughput over time, which reveals different problems: memory leaks, cache degradation, pool connection aging, WAL growth.

SOLUTION: Sustained 100+ RPS load test:
1. Generate steady-state load: 100 requests/sec for 300 seconds (30,000 total operations)
2. Request mix (matching Scenario C from benchmark bead):
   - 40% fetch_inbox, 30% send_message, 15% search, 10% reservations, 5% ack
3. Use 50 worker threads with rate limiter (token bucket at 100 tokens/sec)
4. Measure every 10 seconds:
   - Throughput (actual RPS)
   - Latency percentiles (P50, P95, P99)
   - Error rate
   - Memory RSS
   - SQLite WAL size
   - Cache hit rate
   - Queue depths (WBQ, commit)
5. Assertions:
   - Throughput stays within 10% of target for entire duration (no degradation)
   - P99 latency < 2s throughout (no creeping tail latency)
   - Memory RSS grows < 100MB over 300s (no leak)
   - Zero errors
6. Output time-series data for visualization

FILES: New crates/mcp-agent-mail-db/tests/sustained_load.rs or tests/load/","status":"open","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:26.097923048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:57.601972205Z","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["scale","stress","testing"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.2","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.1.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:57.466571713Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.2","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.2.1","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:57.601897445Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""},{"issue_id":"br-15dv.9.2","depends_on_id":"br-15dv.9","type":"parent-child","created_at":"2026-02-07T03:33:26.097923048Z","created_by":"ubuntu","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}]}
+{"id":"br-15dv.9.2","title":"Sustained 100 RPS load test for 300 seconds with stability assertions","description":"PROBLEM: Current tests measure burst performance (do N operations as fast as possible). They don't test sustained throughput over time, which reveals different problems: memory leaks, cache degradation, pool connection aging, WAL growth.

SOLUTION: Sustained 100+ RPS load test:
1. Generate steady-state load: 100 requests/sec for 300 seconds (30,000 total operations)
2. Request mix (matching Scenario C from benchmark bead):
   - 40% fetch_inbox, 30% send_message, 15% search, 10% reservations, 5% ack
3. Use 50 worker threads with rate limiter (token bucket at 100 tokens/sec)
4. Measure every 10 seconds:
   - Throughput (actual RPS)
   - Latency percentiles (P50, P95, P99)
   - Error rate
   - Memory RSS
   - SQLite WAL size
   - Cache hit rate
   - Queue depths (WBQ, commit)
5. Assertions:
   - Throughput stays within 10% of target for entire duration (no degradation)
   - P99 latency < 2s throughout (no creeping tail latency)
   - Memory RSS grows < 100MB over 300s (no leak)
   - Zero errors
6. Output time-series data for visualization

 {"id":"br-15dv.9.3","title":"Cache thrashing stress test with Zipfian access patterns","description":"PROBLEM: No test for cache behavior when agent count exceeds cache capacity. With default 1000 entries and 1000+ agents, cache thrashes (every insert evicts). Need to verify the LRU overhaul (br-15dv.1.2) actually prevents thrashing.

SOLUTION: Cache thrashing stress test:
1. Configure cache with small capacity (100 entries) for testing
2. Register 500 agents across 10 projects
3. Cycle through all 500 agents, calling resolve_agent for each
4. Measure after each cycle:
   - Cache hit rate (should improve after first cycle with LRU)
   - Resolution latency (cached vs uncached)
   - DB query count (should decrease as cache stabilizes)
5. Assertions:
   - After 2nd cycle: cache hit rate > 80% (working set fits in cache)
   - After 5th cycle: cache hit rate > 95% (LRU stabilized)
   - Thrashing detection: cache eviction rate < 10% of access rate
6. Test with Zipfian distribution (some agents accessed more than others)
   - Popular agents should have near-100% hit rate
7. Test cache coherency: modify agent, verify cache returns updated data

DEPENDS ON: br-15dv.1.2 (LRU cache overhaul)

 {"id":"br-15dv.9.4","title":"Queue saturation stress test for WBQ and commit queue overflow","description":"PROBLEM: No test for WBQ and commit queue behavior when saturated. We need to verify that queues handle overflow gracefully (with new backpressure, not sync fallback).

SOLUTION: Queue saturation stress test:
1. Configure small queues for testing: WBQ_CAPACITY=32, COMMIT_QUEUE=10
2. Burst 200 messages in 1 second (6x WBQ capacity)
3. Measure:
   - WBQ high-water mark (should peak at 32, not overflow)
   - Sync fallback count (should be 0 with backpressure)
   - Drain rate (messages/sec)
   - End-to-end latency for queued vs direct operations
4. Assertions:
   - Zero sync fallbacks (backpressure throttles senders instead)
   - All 200 messages eventually committed to archive
   - No data loss
   - Queue depth returns to 0 within 10 seconds after burst
5. Test commit coalescer batching efficiency:
   - 200 messages should result in <50 git commits (batch size ≥4)
   - Verify batch commit messages contain correct metadata

DEPENDS ON: br-15dv.2.1 (WBQ capacity increase), br-15dv.2.2 (per-project queues)

 {"id":"br-15dv.9.5","title":"Large message payload stress test with size limits and memory tracking","description":"PROBLEM: No test for large message payloads. A message with a 5MB body gets buffered multiple times: tool handler → DB insert → WBQ channel → JSON serialization → archive write. No size limits exist. 256 such messages in WBQ = 1.3GB.

SOLUTION: Large message stress test:
1. Test message size limits (after br-15dv.4.3 implementation):
   - Send message with 1MB body → should succeed
   - Send message with 10MB body → should return INVALID_ARGUMENT
   - Send message with 100MB body → should reject fast (not OOM)
2. Test memory under large message load:
   - Send 100 messages with 500KB bodies concurrently
   - Measure peak RSS during burst
   - Assert: peak RSS < 500MB (reasonable for 100 × 500KB = 50MB payload)
3. Test WBQ behavior with large messages:
   - Queue 50 large messages (500KB each = 25MB in queue)
   - Verify drain completes without OOM
   - Verify archive files are correctly written
4. Test FTS indexing of large bodies:
   - 1MB body indexed in FTS5
   - Search returns correct results
   - Index time < 100ms per message

DEPENDS ON: br-15dv.4.3 (per-message size limits)
